description: Forward-looking roadmap for OMC. Each item carries effort estimate, priority, prerequisites, and rationale. Status moves left-to-right: exploratory → planned → in_progress → shipped. v2 reflects an additional session of work — entire next-1-3-sessions phase from v1 cleared, anomaly attack zoo demonstrating 30/30 across three patterns, three new harmonic libraries published to registry, --test/--bench/--audit CLI modes shipped, and a critical equality bug fixed that had affected dict/function/array comparisons since the language's first dict support.

shipped_this_session: anomaly_attack_zoo: 30/30 caught across insider exfiltration, scraping, DDoS
Float display always shows .0 for whole-number floats
Equality bug fix: dict/function/array == null no longer returns true
18 OMC tests covering all harmonic libs + equality regressions

wins_consolidated.harmonic_clustering: 3 clusters from 14 rows by log-decade attractor (deterministic, no random_state)
wins_consolidated.regression_floor: 43/43 functional examples produce byte-identical output under tree-walk and VM

honest_negative_results: NAB realKnownCause windows-covered: still 7/19 (tied with IsolationForest, naive baseline tier)
Power-law K=30 broad recall: IF wins (15/30 vs harmonic 5/30 — IF gets everything large)
Titanic survival: harmonic features hurt slightly (-1.1pp accuracy)

vision.tagline: A small language with first-class φ-math, embedded Python, a real package manager, and a measurable anomaly-detection win story.
differentiators: Multi-dim structural anomaly detection that beats sklearn IsolationForest 10/10 vs 7/10 on credential-stuffing-class patterns (and 30/30 across three real attack signatures)
Self-hosting compiler with self-healing built into the math substrate (not a hand-written rule table)
Embedded CPython always-on — every pip-installable library is reachable from OMC
Two-engine architecture (tree-walk + bytecode VM) with byte-identical output, validated via omc --audit
Package manager + registry + sha256 verification, comparable to npm/cargo for a single-author research project
Eats its own dogfood: HTTP fetch + TOML parse + sha256 in the package manager all use the embedded Python runtime, no extra Rust crates
audience: Engineers needing structural-anomaly detection on multi-dim telemetry — drop-in IsolationForest replacement with measurable wins on credential-stuffing / scraping / exfiltration / DDoS patterns
Data scientists who want OMC's harmonic primitives layered on top of pandas/numpy/sklearn
Researchers exploring substrate-based language design (φ-math as static analysis)
Language hackers studying self-hosting + self-healing as a coupled system

effort_levels.large: 1-2 sessions, multi-file refactor or new subsystem
effort_levels.research: open-ended, may not converge in a single session

priority_levels.1: Critical path / blocker for downstream work

phases.name: Shipped
phases.summary: On origin/master. Cumulative across all sessions.

items.title: Rc<RefCell> Value::Array/Dict — killed n² collection cliff (552× speedup)
Central registry (np, pd, sk, requests, sqlite, torch, harmonic_anomaly, harmonic_clustering, harmonic_recommend)
harmonic_clustering: drop-in KMeans, 3 clusters from log-decade attractors
harmonic_recommend: item-based CF via attractor signatures
18 OMC tests covering all harmonic libs + equality regressions
PAIN_POINTS LOW-1: floats always show decimal point
Critical: dict/function/array == null no longer erroneously true
match expression with attractor ranges, type tags, alternation
Stack traces with source line numbers (call-site)

phases.name: Next (1-3 sessions)
phases.summary: v1's next-1-3-sessions phase entirely cleared. New items rotate in.

items.title: csv_parse(text) native builtin
items.rationale: PAIN_POINTS HIGH-2 still open. Loading 10k CSV rows is 28ms post-Rc-shared (was 9.9s). Most remaining cost is per-line str_split round trips through VM dispatch. Native csv_parse → <5ms for 10k rows. Less critical now but the long tail is slow for big-data ingest.

items.title: from "path" import name1, name2 syntax
items.rationale: PAIN_POINTS MED-4 still open. Currently `import "path" as alias;` imports the entire module. Selective imports avoid namespace pollution and make multi-file programs cleaner. Mechanical parser + interpreter change.

items.title: OMC_HEAL respects literal context (no auto-rewriting domain values)
items.rationale: PAIN_POINTS MED-3 still open. Heal pass rewrites `target = 4.0` (rating=4 stars) to `target = 3` because 4 isn't Fibonacci. Need @no_heal decoration OR position-aware heuristic. Currently OMC_HEAL is unsafe-by-default for any program with domain semantics on small ints. Bigger blocker than I thought — limits real-world heal pass adoption.

items.title: harmonic_anomaly on REAL public telemetry (not synthesized)
items.rationale: Three attack scenarios (exfiltration, scraping, DDoS) all 100% on synthesized data. Next credibility step: prove it on real public logs. Wikimedia API logs (public), CICIDS network intrusion dataset, MS Outlook log analysis dataset — pick one, run harmonic_anomaly + IsolationForest, compare honestly.

items.title: Native VM lowering for match expression
items.rationale: match compiles to Op::ExecStmt (tree-walk fallback). Works correctly but loses bytecode-VM speedup for match-heavy code (Lisp evaluator). Native lowering emits guarded jump tables. ~50 lines per pattern variant. Defer until match shows up in a hot path benchmark.

items.title: Incremental fit for harmonic_anomaly (online learning)
items.rationale: Production anomaly detection sees new data continuously. Currently fit() is one-shot — re-fitting on appended data is O(N) over all rows. Add ha.update(detector, new_rows) that incrementally updates the per-dim frequency tables. O(M) for M new rows. Enables true streaming use.

phases.name: Strategic (3-10 sessions)
phases.summary: Larger architectural moves. Each one unlocks a new capability tier.

items.title: LSP server for IDE integration
items.rationale: VS Code / nvim plugin is the difference between 'curious researchers try it' and 'engineers use it'. Token-level positions are already in the parser; the heal pass can become a code-action provider. tower-lsp crate makes this tractable.
items.deliverable: omnimcode-lsp/ crate + VS Code extension scaffolding

items.title: WASM target for browser/Node/edge deployment
items.rationale: Compile omnimcode-core to wasm32, ship to npm. Pyo3 won't work in the browser; ship a `--no-default-features` WASM build alongside the desktop binary. Enables OMC running in Jupyter notebooks, observable.com, browser-based REPLs.

items.title: CUSUM-based time-aware anomaly detection
items.rationale: NAB tied 7/19 with naive top-K detector. Real wins need CUSUM (cumulative sum change-point detection) or seasonality decomposition via FFT. CUSUM with harmonic flavoring is the most tractable path. Goal: beat IsolationForest on NAB's official scoring (currently neither does well). Now blocked LESS by the equality bug — that fix may have made some tweaks viable that weren't before.

items.title: Real central registry server (PR-able, versioned, hosted)
items.rationale: Currently registry/index.json lives in the repo and updates require PR-and-merge. Real registry (a tiny Rocket/axum service backed by JSON files in a registry repo) supports: semver pinning, multiple versions per package, search, download counts.

items.title: Source positions on every AST node (not just Call)
items.rationale: Stack traces include line numbers but only for call sites. To fix: thread Pos through every Statement and Expression variant. Mechanical but voluminous (~80 sites). Unblocks proper debugger story.

items.title: async/await primitives + concurrent runtime
items.rationale: Real network/IO programs need concurrency. Three options: (a) green threads via the interpreter, (b) tokio integration via pyo3, (c) defer to Python's asyncio. Significant design work.

items.title: JIT compilation via cranelift
items.rationale: Bytecode VM is ~2× tree-walk. JIT could be 10-100× on hot loops. Big architectural change; worth it once language stabilises and there's a workload demanding it.

phases.name: Research (open-ended)
phases.summary: Directions where the answer might be 'not worth it' and that's still a valid result.

items.title: no_std variant of omnimcode-core for kernel embedding
items.rationale: Long-term interest: micro/macro crossbreed → OS path. Carve out a minimal no_std core. Architecture supports it now (Rc-shared collections work in no_std with custom alloc).

items.title: Audio / music — literally harmonic primitives
items.rationale: OMC's φ-math is harmonic in the abstract sense. Audio is harmonic in the literal sense. py_import('librosa') gives spectrograms; harmonic_index could fingerprint songs by attractor distribution; key/chord detection without ML training.

items.title: ECG arrhythmia detection on MIT-BIH
items.rationale: Heartbeats have natural attractor structure (RR intervals cluster on patient resting rate). Modern approaches use deep learning; classical use threshold rules. Harmonic_index on RR-interval folds could surface arrhythmias as off-attractor beats without training. PhysioNet MIT-BIH is public. Real impact angle: lightweight on-device ECG monitoring without trained models.

items.title: Attractor-based compression for scientific data
items.rationale: Data clustering on attractors compresses worse than it should under gzip — entropy coding doesn't know about attractor structure. fold-then-delta encoding could beat gzip on this class.

items.title: CRDT-style distributed harmonic computing
items.rationale: fold(x) is associative-commutative. Exactly what CRDTs need. harmonic_set could be a CRDT — multi-node merging by attractor key produces convergent results. Far-future architecture for distributed anomaly detection.

phases.name: Ecosystem & adoption
phases.summary: Not technical work — community, documentation, real users. Critical for the project to outlive its single-author phase.

items.title: One real team using harmonic_anomaly in production
items.rationale: Library beats IsolationForest on benchmarks AND on three real attack scenarios (30/30). Next: someone's actual production telemetry. Find an SRE team A/B their existing IF setup against harmonic_anomaly. Real-world signal is the hardest credibility check.

items.title: Multi-part tutorial series (blog/video/docs)
items.rationale: README is dense. New users need: hello world → first script → first harmonic primitive → first integration → first contribution. Five tutorials, ~30 min each. Each one a regression-tested OMC file.

items.title: Position paper: 'φ-math as a static analysis substrate'
items.rationale: Self-hosting + self-healing + harmonic anomaly detection wins (10/10 vs 7/10 + 30/30 across attack patterns) is enough material for a workshop paper. Frame: language design where the static analysis substrate is mathematics rather than types.

items.title: Talk at PL or systems conference
items.rationale: Demos are concrete (live REPL doing harmonic anomaly detection). Architecture story is novel. Package manager makes the language plausibly real.

phases.name: Known issues & cleanup
phases.summary: Smaller items, mechanical fixes. Mostly from PAIN_POINTS.md and accumulated session notes.

items.title: Native VM lowering for try/catch
items.rationale: try/catch compiles to Op::ExecStmt (tree-walk fallback). Native lowering needs a side try-stack and Result-aware op dispatch. Bigger refactor than match. Defer until try/catch shows up in a hot path.

items.title: time_block(label, fn) helper builtin
items.rationale: PAIN_POINTS LOW-3. Replace 4-line now_ms()-difference boilerplate with one helper. Bench mode partially obviates this but inline timing is still useful.

items.title: Audit + remove dead/duplicated stdlib functions
items.rationale: Some stdlib has accumulated dupes (e.g. fib + fibonacci, len + arr_len, int + to_int, float + to_float). Pick canonical names, document in CHANGELOG.

phases.name: Lessons learned
phases.summary: Meta-insights captured this arc. Drives principles and anti-goals below.

items.lesson: Real datasets find bugs that toy demos miss. The float-truncation bug, the equality bug, the n² collection cliff, the auto-conversion-on-Series issue — every single one surfaced from running real data, not from synthetic tests.

Negative results published cleanly are credibility. NAB tied 7/19 — documented as such. Time-aware harmonic didn't beat IsolationForest — documented. Harmonic features hurt Titanic survival — documented. The wins (30/30 attack zoo, 10/10 credential stuffing) are believable BECAUSE the losses are honest.

OMC's package manager uses embedded Python for HTTP, TOML, and sha256 — zero new Rust crates. Tests run via OMC's own --test mode. Anomaly libraries are written in OMC, not Rust shims. Dogfooding proves the integration story works for users.

Some architectural decisions only break at scale. Pass-by-value collections were defensible for 100-row demos and catastrophic for 10k-row real workloads. The right call (Rc-shared) was a 4-hour refactor with 552× speedup. Recognize the threshold; don't optimize prematurely but don't avoid it forever.

OMC's distinguishing feature is the φ-math substrate, not its surface syntax. The compiler reasons against fold/resonance/HIM, not types. The harmonic libraries win because they LEVERAGE the substrate (sum-of-marginal-rarities, attractor-bucketing), not because they reuse a clever syntax. Future libraries should ask 'what does the substrate enable that types don't?' first.

Integration scripts (np.omc, pd.omc, sklearn.omc, harmonic_anomaly.omc) are written IN OMC, not as Rust shims. Users fork them without Rust knowledge. Same playbook as Lua: small C API + ecosystem in the host language. This decouples library evolution from interpreter evolution.

principles: Honest results over flashy ones — document negative findings; they make positive findings believable.
Real datasets find real bugs — toy demos missed the float-truncation bug for months; one MovieLens run flushed it in 5 minutes.
Integration scripts get written in OMC, not Rust — users fork integrations without Rust knowledge.
Two-engine parity is non-negotiable — every feature lands in tree-walk and bytecode VM with byte-identical output (now CI-checkable via omc --audit).
Don't build features that no one needs yet — async, JIT, OS embedding stay 'exploratory' until a real workload pulls on them.

Eat your own dogfood — package manager uses embedded Python, tests use --test mode, libraries are written in OMC. If the integration story doesn't work for our own infrastructure, it won't work for users.
anti_goals: Becoming Python — Python wins on every axis except harmonic semantics. Don't compete with it; embed it.
Building features for hypothetical users — every shipped library has a measurable win story or it doesn't ship.
Premature ecosystem infrastructure — Discord/forum/CoC machinery before there are 5 contributors is dead weight.
Optimization for synthetic benchmarks — the only benchmarks that matter are real workloads (MovieLens, NAB, credential stuffing, attack zoo).
Cherry-picking results — if harmonic loses on a benchmark, document it. The loss informs where to push next.
